perturbation.original_text: In the event the Company is unable to sell a minimum of 1,875,000 Shares on or before June 30, 2007, this Agreement shall terminate and the Company shall cause the Escrow Agent (as defined below) to refund to any persons who have subscribed for any of the Shares the full amount it received from them, without interest, as set forth in the Prospectus; and none of the parties to this Agreement shall have any obligation to the other parties hereunder, except as set forth in this Section 2 and in Sections 8, 10, and 11.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event the Company is unable to sell a minimum of 1,875,000 Shares on or before June 30, 2007, this Agreement shall terminate and the Company may refund to any persons who have subscribed for any of the Shares the amount it received from them, without interest, as determined at the Company's discretion; and none of the parties to this Agreement shall have any obligation to the other parties hereunder, except as set forth in this Section 2 and in Sections 8, 10, and 11.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the guarantee of a full refund to investors if the minimum share sale isn't met by the deadline. The original text clearly states the Escrow Agent *shall* refund the full amount without interest. The modified text replaces this with 'the Company may refund... as determined at the Company's discretion,' thus giving the company the option not to refund, or to only refund a portion, which violates securities regulations regarding full disclosure and investor protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 11 of the Securities Act of 1933 imposes liability on issuers and other parties for false or misleading statements or omissions of material facts in a registration statement. By removing the mandatory refund provision and replacing it with a discretionary one, the contract contradicts the principle that investors should be fully and accurately informed about the risks and potential losses associated with their investment. If the prospectus details a guaranteed refund under specific circumstances, altering the agency agreement to make that refund discretionary creates a material inconsistency, increasing the risk of liability under Section 11.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 2A / SUBCHAPTER I / § 77k Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 77k : Civil liabilities on account of false registration statement Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 2A-SECURITIES AND TRUST INDENTURES SUBCHAPTER I-DOMESTIC SECURITIES Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §77k. Civil liabilities on account of false registration statement (a) Persons possessing cause of action; persons liable In case any part of the registration statement, when such part became effective, contained an untrue statement of a material fact or omitted to state a material fact required to be stated therein or necessary to make the state
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts securities regulations by removing the mandatory refund provision, as the original text specifies that the company 'shall' refund the full amount, while the modified version states the company 'may' refund an amount at its discretion. This directly undermines investor protection and full disclosure principles, which are core tenets of securities law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 77k, highlights civil liabilities for untrue statements or omissions of material facts in a registration statement, which this change could potentially trigger if the prospectus guaranteed a full refund.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will use its best efforts to cause any post-effective amendment to the Registration Statement to be declared effective by the Commission, and will immediately upon receipt of any information concerning the events listed below notify the Agent (i) when the Registration Statement, as amended, has become effective; (ii) of any request by the Commission or any other governmental entity for any amendment or supplement to the Registration Statement; (iii) of the issuance by the Commission or any other governmental agency of any order or other action suspending the Offering or the use of the Registration Statement, the Preliminary Prospectuses, the Prospectus or the Permitted Free Writing Prospectuses, if any; or (iv) of the issuance by the Commission or any state authority of any stop order suspending the effectiveness of the Registration Statement or of the initiation or threat of initiation or threat of any proceedings for that purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will use reasonable efforts to cause any post-effective amendment to the Registration Statement to be declared effective by the Commission, and will notify the Agent of (i) when the Registration Statement, as amended, has become effective.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly weakens the company's commitment to keeping the agent informed about critical regulatory developments. By changing "best efforts" to "reasonable efforts" and removing clauses (ii), (iii), and (iv), the Company reduces its obligation to promptly inform the Agent about requests for amendments, suspension orders, or stop orders related to the Registration Statement. This could leave the Agent in the dark regarding regulatory issues, leading to potential legal violations. Specifically, the omission of prompt notification regarding stop orders could lead to the Agent continuing to solicit investments even when the offering is legally suspended.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits the making of materially false or misleading statements, or omissions of material fact, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By limiting the Company's obligation to inform the Agent about regulatory issues (especially stop orders), the Agent might unknowingly continue selling shares even when it's illegal, thus violating Rule 10b-5. The original text provided a mechanism for the agent to be informed, and the omission of that mechanism significantly raises the agent's risk of unknowingly violating the rule.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits critical notification obligations, specifically regarding requests for amendments, suspension orders, or stop orders related to the Registration Statement. This directly contradicts the purpose of securities regulations like Rule 10b-5, which aims to prevent the sale of securities when it's illegal. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly states that it is unlawful to "omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading...in connection with the purchase or sale of any security." By removing the company's obligation to inform the agent about potential stop orders, the agent might unknowingly sell shares illegally, violating Rule 10b-5. The omission creates a higher risk of non-compliance, as the agent might not be aware of critical regulatory developments.

perturbation.original_text: The Company and the Agent shall transmit all funds received from subscribers to the Escrow Agent by noon of the next business day following receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company and the Agent shall transmit all funds received from subscribers to the Escrow Agent within a reasonable timeframe.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration replaces a clear, defined timeframe ('by noon of the next business day') with a vague term ('reasonable timeframe'). This introduces ambiguity, creating an opportunity for the company or agent to delay transmitting funds to the escrow agent. This can lead to violation of SEC Rule 15c2-4.
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 15c2-4 requires broker-dealers to promptly transmit customer funds received in connection with a contingency offering to a bank or escrow agent. By removing the 'next business day' requirement and replacing it with 'reasonable timeframe,' the contract creates an opportunity to delay fund transmission. A 'reasonable timeframe' is subjective, and delaying the transfer could be interpreted as a violation of the 'promptly transmit' requirement under Rule 15c2-4, potentially leading to regulatory scrutiny and penalties.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.15c2-4 - Transmission or maintenance of payments received in connection with underwritings. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.15c2-4 - Transmission or maintenance of payments received in connection with underwritings. CFR prev | next § 240.15c2-4 Transmission or maintenance of payments received in connection with underwritings. It shall constitute a “fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative act or practice” as used in section 15(c)(2) of the Act, for any broker, dealer or municipal securities dealer participating in any distribution of securities, other than a firm-commitment underwriting , to accept any part of the sale price of any security being distributed unless: (a) The money or other consideration received is promptly transmitted to the persons entitled thereto; or (b) If the distribution is being made on an “all-or-none” basis, or on any other basis which contemplates that payment is not to be made to the person on whose behalf the distribution is being made until some further event or contingency occurs, (1) the money or other consideration received is promptly deposited in a separate bank account, as agent or trustee for the persons who have the beneficial interests therein, until the appropriate event or contingency has occurred, and then the funds are promptly transmitted or returned to the persons entitled thereto, or (2) all such funds are promptly transmitted to a bank which has agreed in writing to hold all such funds in escrow for the persons who have the beneficial interests therein and to transmit or return such funds directly to the persons entitled thereto when the appropriate event or contingency has occurred. [ 41 FR 22826 , June 7, 1976] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the definite timeframe of 'by noon of the next business day' with the vague 'reasonable timeframe'. This contradicts SEC Rule 15c2-4, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, requires prompt transmission of funds. The change introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing delays that would violate the 'promptly transmitted' requirement. The original text aligns with the legal requirement for promptness, while the changed text undermines it.
